Notes on Tokyo, between meetings and side streets
Three days of work-trip detours through Shimokitazawa, a half-day in Daikanyama, and the smallest bar in Shinjuku.

The first morning I left the hotel before anyone in the office expected me to be at a desk. Shimokitazawa was a twenty-minute ride and a different city — older, smaller, leaning. I bought a Carhartt double-knee at a place that didn't have a name on the door and a coffee at a place that did.

## the side streets

The argument for staying in Tokyo three more days than the trip required is that *every* block has a version of itself you'd want to know better. The argument for leaving is that this is true everywhere and you can't keep doing it.

I split the difference. Two short days became one long one, structured like:

- Daikanyama in the morning, the Tsutaya store and walking.
- Lunch in Ebisu, soba, alone, with my phone in my pocket because the room demanded it.
- An afternoon in Shinjuku that became evening without my noticing.

## the bar

Golden Gai is six rows of bars that fit six people each. The one I ended up in fit four. I don't want to write its name down here because it would be a small betrayal — the kind of place that earns its reputation by *not* trending. The bartender played records and didn't talk to me until my second drink, at which point he asked what I do. I said I write software. He nodded like this was a reasonable thing for someone in his bar to do, which I appreciated.

## what I'd do differently

Skip Shibuya Crossing. Skip the observation decks. Eat at the convenience stores without apologizing for it. Bring less.
Why most developer portfolios feel the same
A short piece on the intro / projects / experience / contact formula, and what it costs you.

If you've looked at ten developer portfolios in a row, you've looked at one. The formula is so consistent it's almost ritual: a hero with the person's name and a one-line tagline; a grid of projects with screenshots; a list of jobs in reverse chronological order; a contact section with the same five icons.

There's nothing *wrong* with the formula. It loads quickly, it's recruiter-legible, it does the job. The problem is that the job it does is *not actually the job you want it to do*. The job you want is for someone to come away with a feel for who you are. The job it does is communicate that you are a person who can build a website.

## the substitution

The mistake is treating "portfolio" as a fixed genre — like a résumé — instead of as a personal site that happens to have your work on it. A résumé has rules because everyone is reading it for the same thing. A personal site has no rules because anyone landing on it might be looking for anything.

The interesting move is to *invert* the assumption. Instead of asking "what should a developer portfolio have?", ask "if I had to make a site that captures me, what would it be?". For some people the answer is still a clean grid of projects. For most people it isn't.

## what helps

A few things that consistently improve a personal site, ranked by how much they pay back relative to the effort:

1. **Replace the tagline with a sentence**. Taglines are a meta-promise to summarize you; a sentence actually does it.
2. **Show one thing in depth instead of ten things in passing**. People remember one well-told story; they don't remember a grid.
3. **Let a non-code interest into the room**. Travel, fashion, food, music — whatever you actually care about. It contextualizes the code.
4. **Use real photography, not stock**. Your photos of your trip are infinitely more interesting than the polished landscape stock photo.
5. **Write in your voice**. If you wouldn't say it out loud, don't put it on the site.

None of this requires being a designer. It just requires being willing to make something that doesn't look like everyone else's.
A prosthetic knee that listens to muscle signals
ML work with Alberta BIONIX: classifying surface-EMG patterns to make a prosthetic limb respond to intent in real time.

The problem with a prosthetic knee is that it doesn't know what you're about to do. A standard control loop reacts to forces *after* you start moving — by then the knee is already a half-step behind. The thing we've been working on at Alberta BIONIX takes a different bet: read the electrical signals from the residual leg muscles, and use them to predict the next motion *before* the limb has to commit.

## the input

Eight surface-EMG channels wrapped around the thigh, sampled at 1 kHz. Each window of activity gets turned into a feature vector — RMS, waveform length, slope-sign changes, and a handful of frequency-domain stats. Cheap features, but they hold up.

## the model

A small 1D-CNN, eight inputs wide and roughly a hundred thousand parameters. Trained on labeled data from a stationary rig: sitting, standing, walking, stairs, stumble-recovery. Validation accuracy is in the high 90s on within-subject splits and considerably worse across subjects — which is the real problem and the one we're spending most of our time on now.

## what I learned

Cross-subject generalization in EMG is a different sport from getting good numbers in a paper. Skin impedance, electrode placement, hair, sweat — all of it is real, all of it kills your model in the field. The trick we landed on is a small adaptation step at fit-time: ten seconds of recorded baseline motion, fine-tune the last layer, re-normalize. It doesn't solve the problem, but it gets us from "works in the lab" to "works on the second person who tries it."

## what's next

We're moving from offline classification to streaming. Latency budget: 80 ms from muscle activation to actuator command. Currently sitting around 110 ms, mostly in the Python inference layer. Rewriting the inference path in C++ is the next month of my life.
Notes on building a wardrobe one piece at a time. Streetwear is the entry point, not the destination.

I got into clothes late — about a year ago. The reason was unromantic: my friend kept dressing better than me, and I started paying attention to why. What I found was that the people whose fits I liked best weren't buying *more*; they were buying *more carefully*.

## the rule I gave myself

One piece a month, maximum. Either second-hand or from a brand I actually know something about. No impulse buys, no trend pieces I'll be embarrassed by in a year. The point is to assemble a wardrobe slowly enough that I have to live with each piece before adding to it.

## what's earned a spot

A Stüssy basic tee in cream — the kind you stop noticing because it just works. Carhartt WIP double-knees that look better the more they fade. A pair of New Balance 2002R in a quiet grey that pretty much anything else in the rotation can sit on top of. A vintage Filson cap I found in Tokyo for less than dinner.

## what I keep almost buying

Engineered Garments shirts. Yohji wide trousers (definitely too much commitment, definitely will own a pair eventually). One specific Aimé Leon Dore hoodie that I've been talking myself out of for six weeks.

## the through-line

Streetwear is a doorway, not a room. What I'm actually drawn to is *functional, slightly oversized, slightly worn-looking*. That description fits a Carhartt jacket and an Engineered Garments shirt and an old Filson bag, and probably nothing currently on the wall at H&M.

I'll know I'm getting somewhere when I stop having to think about what to put on.
